coredata.dc:title: Incorporating graphene quantum dots boosts thermoelectric performance of PEDOT:PSS films

Liu, Qingfeng
coredata.dc:description: Conductive polymers, particularly poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):(styrene sulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS), are gaining significant attention in flexible thermoelectrics due to their excellent flexibility, low thermal conductivity, and structural stability. However, the electrical conductivity of pristine PEDOT:PSS (below 1 S cm−1) limits its power factor. Although polar solvent doping can enhance electrical conductivity, such methods often require complex acid-based post-treatments, compromising material stability and safety. Here, we report highly conductive graphene quantum dot (GQDs)-incorporated PEDOT:PSS films, fabricated via a solvent-free process. The incorporation of trace amounts of GQDs into the PSS and PEDOT matrix enhances the π-π conjugation within the PEDOT matrix, achieving a electrical conductivity of 3027 S cm−1 and a power factor of up to 207.8 μW m−1 K−2 at room temperature. The solvent-free process preserves film flexibility, making them ideal for flexible thermoelectric devices. These findings present a scalable strategy to enhance the performance of organic thermoelectric materials.

Thermoelectric
Film
Flexible device

Home-made thermoelectric device of PEDOT:PSS films

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Ting Wu and Xiao-Lei Shi contributed equally to this work. This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52272040 ), the State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering (SKL-MCE-23A04), and the Jiangsu Specially-Appointed Professor Program. ZGC thanks financial support from the Australian Research Council , HBIS-UQ Innovation Centre for Sustainable Steel project, and the QUT Capacity Building Professor Program. This work acknowledges the National Computational Merit Allocation Scheme 2024, supported by the National Computational Infrastructure , for providing computational resources and service. This work was enabled using the Central Analytical Research Facility hosted by the Institute for Future Environments at QUT.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Introduction of introducing graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to boost thermoelectric performance of poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):poly (styrenesulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS) films. (a) Structure diagram of PEDOT:PSS (H, C, O, S atoms are shown in white, grey, blue, and red, respectively) and GQDs. (b) Room-temperature power factor (S 2 σ) and Seebeck coefficient (S) of this work and compares the results between PEDOT:PSS films incorporated with different GQDs and those in reported references [27,32–44]. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Thermoelectric performance of PEDOT:PSS films with and without GQDs at room-temperature. (a) S, (b) electrical conductivity (σ), and (c) S 2 σ of PEDOT:PSS films incorporated with different mass ratios (0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4, and 0.5 wt%) of GQDs. (d) Comparison of thermoelectric performance between pristine PEDOT:PSS film and 0.1 wt% GQDs-incorporated PEDOT:PSS films (0.1 GPP).

Characterizations of PEDOT:PSS films with and without GQDs. (a) X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of pristine PEDOT:PSS and 0.1 wt% GQDs-incorporated PEDOT:PSS films with and without ethylene glycol (EG) treatment. (b) Raman spectra, (c) full-width at the half of the maximum (FWHM) and peak position and (d) ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra of pristine PEDOT:PSS and 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4 and 0.5 wt% of GQDs-incorporated PEDOT:PSS films. Inset in (d) is correlative UV–vis absorption spectra from 200 to 280 nm.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of both pristine and GQD-incorporated PEDOT:PSS films. High-resolution XPS patterns of (a) original PEDOT:PSS and (b-f) 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4 and 0.5 wt% of GQDs-incorporated PEDOT:PSS films for S2p.

Surface morphology of PEDOT:PSS films with and without GQDs. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of (a) pristine and (b) 0.1 wt% GQDs-incorporated PEDOT:PSS films. The three-dimensional (3D) atomic force microscopy (AFM) images of (c) pristine and (d) 0.1 wt% GQDs-incorporated PEDOT:PSS films. Cross-sectional SEM images of (e) pristine and (f) 0.1 wt% GQDs-incorporated PEDOT:PSS films.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 6

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Flexibility and deviceization of PEDOT:PSS films. (a) Normalized resistance (R/R 0) of 0.1 wt% GQDs-incorporated PEDOT:PSS film as a function of bending radius. The inset shows a photograph of the flexible film being bent. (b) R/R 0 of the same film as a function of bending cycles. The inset displays a photograph of the film enduring repeated bending. (c) Schematic of a thermoelectric device comprising four 0.1 wt% GQDs-incorporated PEDOT:PSS film legs (p-type) connected by nickel wires (n-type). Liquid metal is applied at the joints to minimize contact resistance, and the substrate is polyimide (PI) film. (d) Photographs of (Ⅰ) the thermoelectric device, (Ⅱ) open-circuit voltage (V OC) measurement at ΔT = 5 K and (Ⅲ) V OC measurement on a human arm as a heat.

ce:figure.ce:source: Source. (e) Voltage (V) vs. loading current (I load) for the device at ΔT = 10 K and 5 K. (f) Output power (P) vs. I load under the same ΔT conditions

head.ce:title: Incorporating graphene quantum dots boosts thermoelectric performance of PEDOT:PSS films

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering, College of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing 211816 China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Conductive polymers, particularly poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):(styrene sulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS), are gaining significant attention in flexible thermoelectrics due to their excellent flexibility, low thermal conductivity, and structural stability. However, the electrical conductivity of pristine PEDOT:PSS (below 1 S cm−1) limits its power factor. Although polar solvent doping can enhance electrical conductivity, such methods often require complex acid-based post-treatments, compromising material stability and safety. Here, we report highly conductive graphene quantum dot (GQDs)-incorporated PEDOT:PSS films, fabricated via a solvent-free process. The incorporation of trace amounts of GQDs into the PSS and PEDOT matrix enhances the π-π conjugation within the PEDOT matrix, achieving a electrical conductivity of 3027 S cm−1 and a power factor of up to 207.8 μW m−1 K−2 at room temperature. The solvent-free process preserves film flexibility, making them ideal for flexible thermoelectric devices. These findings present a scalable strategy to enhance the performance of organic thermoelectric materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the increasing demand for clean and renewable energy solutions, thermoelectric materials and devices, which directly convert heat into electricity, have garnered significant attention [1]. The efficiency of thermoelectric devices is closely tied to the performance of the materials [2], commonly evaluated using the dimensionless figure of merit (ZT), defined as ZT = S 2 σT/κ, where S (μV K−1) is the Seebeck coefficient, σ (S cm−1) is the electrical conductivity, κ (W m−1 K−1) is the thermal conductivity, and T (K) is the absolute temperature. The term S 2 σ is referred to as the power factor. Inorganic bulk materials such as GeTe [3], SnSe [4], and PbTe [5] have achieved high ZT values (> 2) at elevated temperatures (> 600 K) through carrier concentration (n) tuning via doping or alloying and reduction of κ by introducing lattice defects for enhanced phonon scattering. However, the toxicity and rigidity of these materials hinder their suitability for flexible and wearable applications at room temperature [6].

Compared to inorganic thermoelectric materials [7], conductive polymers have emerged as promising alternatives due to their flexibility, cost-effectiveness, and environmental compatibility [8]. Among these, poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):(styrene sulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS) is particularly notable for its low κ (ranging between 0.17 and 0.37 W m−1 K−1) [9,10], tunable σ, and environmental stability, making it a strong candidate for flexible thermoelectric applications, including power generation, sensing, and wearable device [11]. Research on polymers typically focuses on enhancing the S 2 σ, as the κ is challenging to evaluate accurately in such systems [12]. One major strategy involves decoupling the inherent correlation between the S and σ, which facilitates the adjustment of doping levels through successive post-treatment processes [13–16]. Doping approaches for PEDOT:PSS include (i) removing the insulating PSS layer to enhance delocalized bipolarons via treatments with polar solvents or acids [17–19], or (ii) dedoping the PEDOT chains to shift carriers into polarons and neutral states [20–22]. However, conventional dedopant often leaves residual counterions that distort the PEDOT backbone, transforming the linear, charge-transport-favorable quinoid structure into a less conductive benzoid form, leading to a drop in σ [14,23]. Therefore, developing novel doping methods that simultaneously enhance both σ and S while preserving PEDOT’s structural integrity remains a critical challenge [24].

While polar solvents and acids improve σ, these methods require complex post-treatments, introducing risks to stability and safety. Quantum dots (QDs) are zero-dimensional (0D) derivatives of graphene, consisting of nanostructures with diameters typically in the range of 2–20 nm [25]. Their quantum-confined size imparts unique properties, such as energy level discretization and altered optical characteristics. Recently, QDs materials have emerged as promising additives to enhance the thermoelectric performance of PEDOT:PSS [26]. QDs, especially carbon-based variants, are low-temperature-processable and leverage quantum confinement effects to modulate electrical transport properties [27,28]. By adjusting the energy level offsets between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of PEDOT:PSS and QDs, energy filtering can be achieved, improving S 2 σ [28]. However, the enhancement achieved with QDs remains limited, and solution-processing methods associated with QDs addition often inherited the drawbacks of solvent treatments. To overcome these limitations, it is crucial to identify more efficient QDs that significantly boost thermoelectric performance and to elucidate their mechanisms of action. Additionally, innovative approaches are needed to integrate QDs into PEDOT:PSS without relying on solvent treatments, ensuring the preservation of flexibility, structural stability and flexibility.

In this study, graphene QDs (GQDs) were selected to form a composite with PEDOT:PSS due to their excellent solubility in water containing little N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) solvents and their good miscibility with PEDOT:PSS. GQDs embedded within the PSS shell act as charge percolation pathways between PEDOT-rich particles, enhancing charge transport in the composite films. Fig. 1
a illustrates the chemical structure of PEDOT:PSS, where PEDOT and PSS are held together by electrostatic interactions. Upon GQDs doping, π-π conjugation interactions arise between PEDOT and GQDs, alongside electrostatic interactions between PSS and GQDs. In this work, GQDs were directly mixed with a PEDOT:PSS solution, and thin films were fabricated via spin coating to assess their thermoelectric properties ( Fig. S1 ). Specific experimental details are given in the supplementary information. The addition of a small amount of GQDs enabled a significant enhancement in σ without requiring any additional post-treatment. This resulted in optimized thermoelectric performance, achieving a S 2 σ of 207.8 μW m−1 K−2 and a σ of 3027 S cm−1. Typically, the intrinsic κ of PEDOT:PSS is low, with reported values around 0.3 W m−1 K−1 in current studies [29–31]. Based on this, we estimate the ZT value of the film to be approximately 0.17. Fig. 1 b highlights the room-temperature S 2 σ achieved in this study compared to previously reported GQDs-incorporated PEDOT:PSS systems (details summarized in Table S1 in the Supporting Information), demonstrating the strong competitiveness of the proposed approach [27,32–44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermoelectric performance of GQDs-incorporated PEDOT:PSS films
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the optimal doping concentration, we first doped PEDOT:PSS with various concentrations of GQDs (0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4, and 0.5 wt%) and prepared thin films to measure their thermoelectric properties at room temperature. Fig. 1 a-c show the S, σ, and S 2 σ of the PEDOT:PSS/GQDs composite films. It can be observed that S initially increases and then decreases with increasing GQDs content, indicating the formation of interfaces between GQDs and PEDOT:PSS. Similarly, σ shows an increasing-then-decreasing trend, with σ exceeding 2000 S cm−1 in the 0.1 ∼ 0.3 wt% doping range. This is comparable to other PEDOT:PSS films treated with formamide [45]. We included a sample with 0.1 wt% DMF content as a blank test to eliminate any potential influence of DMF on the thermoelectric properties of the films. Furthermore, the films were subjected to prolonged high-temperature drying so that the solvent was completely removed. The measured S and σ of the DMF-doped PEDOT:PSS film were 19.09 μV K−1 and 215.49 S cm−1, respectively, resulting in a S 2 σ of 7.85 μW m−1 K−2. Fig. 1 d demonstrates that doping PEDOT:PSS films with 0.1 wt% GQDs increased S from 23.4 to 26.2 μV K−1, while σ was significantly enhanced to 3027 S cm−1. This led to an optimized S 2 σ of 207.8 μW m−1 K−2, exceeding that of pristine films by more than 200 times. The thermoelectric properties of PEDOT:PSS films with 0.1 wt% GQDs as a function of temperature are presented in Fig. S2 . See (Fig. 2 ).

In a comparative study, we tested two commercial PEDOT:PSS materials (PH1000 and AI 4083) combined with GQDs. These materials have PEDOT-to-PSS ratios of 1:2.5 and 1:6. The σ of PEDOT:PSS (PH1000) increased significantly from 2.46 to 3027 S cm−1, while that of PEDOT:PSS (AI4083) rose from 0.007 to 60.47 S cm−1. This result highlights the ability of GQDs to embed effectively even at high PSS ratios, facilitating the formation of conductive channels between PEDOT and PSS, thereby optimizing the thermoelectric properties of PEDOT:PSS materials. As shown in Fig. S3 , the thermoelectric performance of both PEDOT:PSS materials was improved by GQDs doping. When the GQDs doping concentration exceeded 0.4 wt%, σ sharply decreased, which can be attributed to the confinement effect of GQDs. Further treatment of GQD-incorporated films with ethylene glycol (EG) ( Fig. S4 ) revealed a decrease in overall thermoelectric performance. With increasing GQDs content, both σ and S declined, suggesting that the interfaces formed between GQDs and PEDOT:PSS were disrupted. Typically, EG removes PSS to enhance σ. However, this phenomenon indicates that GQDs may interact with PSS and be removed by EG. Our work demonstrates that doping with trace amounts of GQDs can achieve high thermoelectric performance without any post-treatment, simplifying the process of achieving high-performance PEDOT:PSS films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface morphology and structure characterization of PEDOT:PSS films
ce:section.ce:para:
To further investigate how GQDs improve the thermoelectric performance of PEDOT:PSS films, X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy, and ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectroscopy were employed [46]. Fig. 3
a presents the XRD patterns of pristine PEDOT:PSS films, 0.1 wt% GQD-incorporated PEDOT:PSS films, and 0.1 wt% GQD-incorporated films post-treated with EG. The XRD results reveal a characteristic peak of PEDOT:PSS around 4.7°, consistent with previous studies [13,47]. A peak at 24° corresponds to the quartz substrate. The intensity of the diffraction peak decreases for GQD-incorporated films compared to the pristine ones, indicating enhanced amorphous morphology. EG post-treatment restores the crystallinity of the GQD-incorporated films to a level comparable to the pristine films, suggesting that GQDs incorporation does not promote PEDOT rearrangement but instead increases disorder within the PEDOT:PSS matrix. This implies interactions between GQDs and PSS. Fig. 3 b-c show the Raman spectra of PEDOT:PSS films incorporated with 0.1 ∼ 0.5 wt% GQDs. The Raman peaks between 1400 and 1500 cm−1 are attributed to the symmetric Cα = Cβ stretching of PEDOT. For the pristine PEDOT:PSS films, the characteristic peak of PEDOT appears at 1424 cm−1. Upon incorporating with 0.1 wt% GQDs, the peak shifts slightly to 1437 cm−1. As the GQDs concentration increases, the high-wavenumber peak undergoes a blue shift, indicating a structural transition of PEDOT chains from a coiled (benzoid) to an extended (quinoid) structure [48], favorable for carrier transport [49]. The full width at half maximum (FWHM) in Fig. 3 c initially broadens and then narrows with increasing GQDs content, suggesting reduced crystallinity due to GQD-PEDOT interactions. Additionally, the improved uniformity with increased GQDs content confirms that small concentrations of GQDs enhance both σ and the S by influencing interactions between PSS and the PEDOT matrix. Fig. 3 d displays the UV–Vis absorption spectra of PEDOT:PSS films incorporated with 0.1 ∼ 0.5 wt% GQDs. The enhanced absorption intensity in the 200 ∼ 400 nm range reflects GQDs contributions. PEDOT neutral states and polaron absorption are evident around 600 and 900 nm, respectively. The disturbance at 850 nm is due to light fluctuations during testing. Compared to the untreated PEDOT:PSS, the GQD-incorporated samples show no significant changes in absorption near 600 nm but exhibit weaker absorption at 900 nm, indicating a bipolaron-to-polaron transition in some PEDOT chains. The absence of a distinct π-π* band for neutral PEDOT states suggests that the neutral state is not predominant. Therefore, the PEDOT chains are only partially reduced, maintaining high σ. The n and carrier mobility (μ) of the initial PEDOT:PSS were determined to be 9.6 × 1020 cm−3 and 0.2452 cm2 V−1 s−1, respectively. After the incorporation of GQDs, the n in the PEDOT:PSS films decreased to 5.2 × 1020 cm−3, which is likely attributed to the energy filtering effect induced by the embedding of quantum dots [50]. This effect, established by the potential walls and wells within the PEDOT:PSS films, further enhanced the S. Additionally, the μ increased to 0.4732 cm2 V−1 s−1, reflecting the improved structural domain penetration within the conducting PEDOT. This enhancement in μ suggests that the introduction of GQDs preserves the microstructure of polymer by increasing the relative quinone structure of the PEDOT chains, as corroborated by Raman spectroscopy results. These variations in n and μ provide valuable insights into the impact of post-treatments on the thermoelectric properties of PEDOT:PSS samples.

In order to clarify the effects of GQDs on the interactions within PEDOT:PSS, we investigated the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of both pristine and GQD-incorporated PEDOT:PSS films, as shown in Fig. 4
a-f. The spectra in Fig. S5-6 correspond to the C 1 s and O 1 s regions of the PEDOT:PSS films, respectively. The O 1 s peak for pristine PEDOT:PSS is located at 532.7 eV, attributed to the C-O bond in PEDOT. The C 1 s peaks for pristine PEDOT:PSS appear at 286.3 and 284.7 eV, primarily resulting from C-O and C-C bonds. The S2p1/2 and S2p3/2 peaks of PEDOT:PSS were deconvoluted into three components. The broad peaks at high binding energies (166 ∼ 170 eV) correspond to the S2p3/2 and S2p1/2 peaks of sulfonate sulfur atoms in PSS [51]. The peaks at 169.4 and 168.1 eV originate from PSS–H+, while those at 169.3 and 168.1 eV are attributed to PSS–Na+ [52]. The S2p3/2 and S2p1/2 peaks of sulfur atoms in PEDOT are located at lower binding energies (162 ∼ 166 eV), with peaks at 165.4 and 164.2 eV [51]. The area of the S2p peak for PSS is similar to that for PEDOT, and the ratio of the peak intensities remains relatively unchanged, indicating that the insulating PSS component has not been removed. The peak centers of the S2p orbitals in PEDOT:PSS shift after the incorporation of GQDs, indicating that the embedded GQDs interact with both PSS and PEDOT. Some of the anions (SO3−) in the PSS molecules redistribute their charges among different cations (H+ and Na+), altering the ratio of PSS−H+ and PSS−Na+. This redistribution changes the microenvironment of PSS, resulting in a variation in the ratio of PSS−H+ and PSS−Na+. Consequently, more PSS becomes enriched either on the surface of the film or in specific regions, which affects the peak intensity associated with PSS−H+ and PSS−Na+.

To understand the high thermoelectric performance of the PEDOT:PSS films, we conducted a comprehensive structural and morphological characterization. Fig. 5
a-b show scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the pristine PEDOT:PSS films and the PEDOT:PSS films incorporated with 0.1 wt% GQDs. Fig. S7 displays SEM images of the surface and cross-section of PEDOT:PSS films incorporated with 0.2 ∼ 0.6 wt% GQDs. After incorporating with GQDs, there was no significant change in the surface morphology. Fig. 5 c-d are three-dimensional (3D) atomic force microscopy (AFM) phase images of the above samples, providing a more intuitive view of their morphology. The roughness of the films increased after incorporating with GQDs, which corresponds to the Raman results. The introduction of a small concentration of GQDs also increased the amorphous morphology of the films. The adhesive interaction between the PEDOT layers after incorporating with GQDs suggests that the GQDs are connected with PSS, as shown in Fig. 5 e-f.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Home-made thermoelectric device of PEDOT:PSS films
ce:section.ce:para: In terms of the flexibility, Fig. 6 a-b show the relationship between the normalized resistance (R/R 0) of the 0.1 wt% GQDs-incorporated PEDOT:PSS films and the bending radius and number of bending cycles, respectively. Clearly, as the bending radius increases, R/R 0 tends to stabilize. In addition to changing the bending radius, we also performed multiple bending tests. The film resistance was not affected by the number of bending cycles, indicating good stability and flexibility.

In order to investigate the practical application potential of the prepared PEDOT:PSS films, we fabricated a flexible thermoelectric device (F-TED) composed of 0.1 wt% GQDs-incorporated films. Fig. 6 c shows the structure of the designed F-TED, which consists of four 0.1 wt% GQDs-incorporated PEDOT:PSS films as the p-type legs. We used nickel wires (Ni) to connect the PEDOT:PSS films, which were bonded together using liquid metal bonding and then reinforced with copper tape for anchoring. Copper wires were used to connect the two sides of the device to the instrument for testing. Fig. 6 d shows the optical image of the F-TED with a polyimide (PI) film substrate. The insert in Fig. 6d shows a test photo of the F-TED attached to a human hand. When the temperature difference (ΔT) between the ambient temperature and the human arm is 5 K, the output voltage of the F-TED is 0.136 V. Fig. 6 e-f show the measured voltage (V) and power (P) of the F-TED under different ΔTs (10 and 15 K) as the load current (I load) varies. The V decreases as I load increases, and the P peaks around 1.5 μA. Additionally, both V and P increase as ΔT increases. At ΔT of 15 K, a high P value of 1.66 μW was obtained, with a maximum power density of 54.6 μW cm−2. This demonstrates the potential for powering low-grade wearable electronics.

In this study, incorporating trace amounts of GQDs can effectively increase the thermoelectric properties of PEDOT:PSS films with good conductivity and mechanical stability. The σ of the GQD-doped PEDOT:PSS films exceeded 3000 S cm−1 without requiring post-processing, achieving a high S 2 σ of > 200 μW m−1 K−2. This performance improvement is primarily attributed to the hydrophilic nature of the GQDs and their strong π-π interactions with the PEDOT:PSS matrix. Moreover, the flexibility of the PEDOT:PSS films was well-preserved with GQDs incorporation. The assembled F-TEDs based on these films demonstrated an output power density of 54.6 μW cm−2 at a ΔT of 15 K. This solvent-free method improves the efficiency of fabricating advanced thermoelectric films, eliminating complex post-treatment steps. The integration of GQDs into PEDOT:PSS provides a promising pathway for tuning thermoelectric properties while maintaining stability and scalability, paving the way for efficient, sustainable organic thermoelectric materials for energy applications.

Ting Wu: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xiao-Lei Shi: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yu-Yu Deng: Methodology, Formal analysis. Yuan-Meng Liu: Formal analysis. Min Zhu: Formal analysis. Wei-Di Liu: Supervision, Formal analysis. Meng Li: Investigation, Formal analysis. Fang Yue: Investigation. Pei Huang: Supervision, Formal analysis. Zhi-Gang Chen: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Qingfeng Liu: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Ting Wu and Xiao-Lei Shi contributed equally to this work. This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52272040), the State Key Laboratory of Materials-Oriented Chemical Engineering (SKL-MCE-23A04), and the Jiangsu Specially-Appointed Professor Program. This work acknowledges the National Computational Merit Allocation Scheme 2024, supported by the National Computational Infrastructure, for providing computational resources and service. This work was enabled using the Central Analytical Research Facility hosted by the Institute for Future Environments at QUT.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2025.160219.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.G. Olabi, M.A. Abdelkareem, Renewable energy and climate change, Renew. Sust. Energ. Rev., 158 (2022) 112111.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Thermoelectric materials and applications in buildings

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Sun, C. Du, G. Chen, Thermoelectric materials and applications in buildings, Prog. Mater. Sci., 149 (2025) 101402.
